column4: Question ID (Mandatory and Unique, for example: 1.1, 1.2, Etc)
column5: Question Title (Mandatory)
column6: Question Description (Optional)
column7: Answer Type (Mandatory, one of the following options: 1 for Open Answer, 2 for Dropdown - Single Select, 3 for Dropdown - Single Select - Open answer, 4 for Date, 5 for Dropdown - Multiple Select, 6 for Dropdown - Multiple Select - Open answer)
column8: Dropdown Options (Mandatory if column G is Dropdown. List options for the dropdown separated by a pipe character, for example: Yes|No|Undefined)
column9: DropDown Warnings (Optional, for every option on column H you can define a message, for example: Great|Oh|Think twice)
column10: Dropdown Scoring (Mandatory, set this to 1 if you want to enable scoring for this question. Otherwise set to 0)
column11: Dropdown Option Score (Mandatory if J is set to 1, set the score of the question as an integer positive number, for example 10)
column12: Dropdown Option Score Multiplier (Mandatory if J is set to 1. For every option of the dropdown in column H define an integer positive number that will be multiplied by the value on column K, for example: 1|2|10)
column13: Dropdown Option Show another Question (Optional and only possible if column G is dropdown.For every option in column H you can define one or more question IDs from column D to be shown when the option is selected. For example: 1.2 1.6||6.7 would display questions 1.2 and 1.6 if the first option is selected, none if the second is selected and 6.7 if the third option is selected)
column14: Question Comments & Attachments (Mandatory, select 0 if you do not want to show this option or 1 if you want to let users provide comments and attachments for each question)

column5: Is there a team or individual responsible for network security?
column6: This should include firewalls, routers, switches, wireless controllers, VPN gateways, load balancers, and other network devices.

column5: Provide the names of the team members or individuals responsible for network security.
column6: Include internal and outsourced responsibilities, if applicable.

column5: Which network components are in scope for this assessment? Select one or more than one.
column6: Select every component that supports company connectivity, production services, office networks, cloud connectivity, or customer-facing services.
column8: Firewalls|Routers|Switches|Wireless networks|VPN gateways|Load balancers|Cloud network components|Cabling|Network data rooms|Other

column5: Do you maintain an inventory of network devices?
column6: The inventory should include firewalls, routers, switches, wireless access points, controllers, VPN devices, and cloud network components.

column5: Which tool or record is used to maintain the network device inventory?
column6: Common examples include a CMDB, network monitoring tool, asset register, cloud inventory, or configuration management platform.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove network devices are inventoried?
column6: Typical evidence includes CMDB exports, network diagrams, monitoring tool exports, ownership records, or firewall/router inventories.

column5: Do network devices require authenticated administrative access?
column6: This includes local console access, SSH, web administration, cloud management portals, and controller access.

column5: Is the authentication mechanism centralized against LDAP, AD, Etc or these are local accounts?
column6: Examples include directory authentication, TACACS+, RADIUS, SSO, privileged access management, or equivalent controls.

column5: Are accounts regularly reviewed to ensure the right people has the correct access?
column6: This should apply to privileged access through VPN, management portals, bastion hosts, and cloud consoles where supported.

column5: Are default, shared, and unused administrative accounts removed or disabled?
column6: Shared or emergency accounts should be approved, controlled, logged, and reviewed periodically.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove administrative access is reviewed?
column6: Auditors typically like to see AAA configuration, access control groups, MFA settings, admin account lists, and privileged access reviews.

column5: Do you maintain standard configuration baselines for network devices?
column6: Baselines should define approved services, management protocols, password settings, encryption, logging, SNMP settings, and unnecessary service restrictions.

column5: Which network device types have approved configuration baselines? Select one or more than one.
column6: Select all device families for which a documented secure configuration exists.
column8: Firewalls|Routers|Switches|Wireless access points|VPN gateways|Load balancers|Cloud network services|Other

column5: Are network device configurations reviewed against the approved baseline?
column6: This can be performed manually, through configuration management, vulnerability scanning, or compliance monitoring tools.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove secure configurations are defined and checked?
column6: Typical evidence includes hardening standards, configuration templates, compliance reports, screenshots, or configuration review tickets.

column5: Are changes to network devices managed through a formal change management process?
column6: This should include firewall rules, routing changes, VLANs, VPN settings, wireless changes, and management access changes.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove network changes are controlled?
column6: Approval should consider business need, security impact, rollback plan, and implementation timing.

column5: Is network traffic logged or monitored for security purposes?
column6: This should include firewall logs, VPN logs, wireless logs, IDS/IPS events, DNS logs, proxy logs, or cloud network flow logs where applicable.

column5: Which types of network traffic or events are logged? Select one or more than one.
column6: Select the logs collected and available for security monitoring or investigation.
column8: Firewall allow/deny traffic|VPN connections|Wireless authentication|DNS queries|Proxy traffic|IDS/IPS events|Cloud flow logs|Administrative access|Other

column5: Are network logs sent to a centralized logging or SIEM platform?
column6: Centralized logging helps detect suspicious traffic, investigate incidents, and preserve evidence.

column5: How long are network security logs retained?
column6: Include hot retention, archived retention, and whether logs are protected from unauthorized modification or deletion.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove network logs are collected and reviewed?
column6: Typical evidence includes SIEM dashboards, sample log searches, alert rules, retention settings, and incident tickets linked to network events.

column2: Network Device Patching and Vulnerability Management
column5: Are network devices patched or upgraded under a defined process?
column6: The process should cover firmware, operating systems, security updates, controller updates, and emergency fixes.

column2: Network Device Patching and Vulnerability Management
column5: Are patching timelines defined based on severity?
column6: The SLA should define how quickly critical, high, medium, and low severity issues are assessed and remediated.

column2: Network Device Patching and Vulnerability Management
column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove network device patching is managed?
column6: This should include authenticated scans where possible and manual validation for devices that cannot be scanned safely.

column5: Do you track end-of-life and end-of-support dates for network devices?
column6: This should include hardware support, firmware support, software support, and vendor security advisory coverage.

column5: Are end-of-life network devices replaced, upgraded, or risk accepted before support expires?
column6: Unsupported devices should not remain in production without documented risk acceptance and compensating controls.

column5: Are exceptions for unsupported network devices formally approved and time limited?
column6: Include the business reason, security risk, compensating controls, target remediation date, and owner.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove end-of-life devices are managed?
column6: Typical evidence includes lifecycle registers, vendor notices, replacement plans, risk acceptances, and decommissioning tickets.

column5: Are Wi-Fi networks secured using strong authentication and encryption?
column6: Examples include WPA2-Enterprise, WPA3-Enterprise, certificate-based access, RADIUS, or equivalent controls.

column5: Are guest Wi-Fi networks separated from corporate networks?
column6: Guest Wi-Fi should not provide direct access to internal systems unless specifically approved and controlled.

column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove Wi-Fi networks are secured?
column6: Typical evidence includes wireless controller settings, SSID configuration, authentication settings, segmentation rules, and guest network isolation evidence.

column2: Cabling and Network Data Room Security
column5: Are network data rooms, closets, and cabinets physically secured/enclosed?
column6: This includes offices, server rooms, wiring closets, shared buildings, and locations where network equipment is installed.

column2: Cabling and Network Data Room Security
column5: Is access to network data rooms restricted to authorized personnel?
column6: Access should be controlled through keys, badges, locks, visitor controls, or other physical access controls.

column2: Cabling and Network Data Room Security
column5: Is network cabling protected from unauthorized access or tampering?
column6: This should include patch panels, exposed cables, network ports in public areas, and unused wall ports.

column2: Cabling and Network Data Room Security
column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove cabling and data room security is controlled from non-authorized personel?
column6: Auditors typically like to see access lists, photos, badge access logs, visitor logs, cabinet lock records, and port control procedures.

column3: This chapter reviews how inbound network traffic is restricted, approved, segmented, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether the target is an office, datacenter, cloud, or hosted environment.
column5: Do you restrict inbound network traffic by default?
column6: Inbound traffic should be blocked unless there is a documented business need, approved rule, defined owner, and controlled source and destination.

column3: This chapter reviews how inbound network traffic is restricted, approved, segmented, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether the target is an office, datacenter, cloud, or hosted environment.
column5: Which inbound traffic types are allowed? Select one or more than one.
column6: Include traffic entering from the Internet, third parties, remote users, cloud services, offices, datacenters, hosted services, or other networks.
column8: Internet to internal systems|Third-party or partner connections|Remote access or VPN traffic|Cloud service connections|Inter-site traffic|Management traffic|Monitoring or backup traffic|Other

column3: This chapter reviews how inbound network traffic is restricted, approved, segmented, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether the target is an office, datacenter, cloud, or hosted environment.
column5: Which controls are used for inbound traffic? Select one or more than one.
column6: Select the controls used to restrict, inspect, segment, or monitor inbound traffic before it reaches internal or production networks.
column8: Default deny firewall rules|Approved source allow-listing|Port and protocol restrictions|DMZ or segmented zones|WAF or reverse proxy|VPN or private connectivity|IDS/IPS inspection|Temporary rules with expiry dates|Periodic rule reviews|Other

column3: This chapter reviews how inbound network traffic is restricted, approved, segmented, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether the target is an office, datacenter, cloud, or hosted environment.
column5: Are inbound traffic rules reviewed periodically?
column6: The review should confirm business need, owner, source, destination, port, protocol, expiry date, change reference, and whether the rule is still required.

column3: This chapter reviews how inbound network traffic is restricted, approved, segmented, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether the target is an office, datacenter, cloud, or hosted environment.
column5: Are temporary inbound access rules given an expiry date and removed after use?
column6: This includes vendor access, incident response access, migration access, testing access, and temporary troubleshooting access.

column3: This chapter reviews how inbound network traffic is restricted, approved, segmented, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether the target is an office, datacenter, cloud, or hosted environment.
column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove inbound traffic is controlled?
column6: Typical evidence includes firewall rule exports, approvals, change tickets, rule review records, network diagrams, DMZ diagrams, WAF configuration, VPN configuration, SIEM alerts, and exception records.

column3: This chapter reviews how outbound network traffic is restricted, approved, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether it originates from users, servers, infrastructure, cloud, or hosted environments.
column5: Do you control outgoing network traffic?
column6: Controls should define which users, systems, servers, infrastructure components, guest networks, cloud workloads, and third-party connections can initiate outbound traffic.

column3: This chapter reviews how outbound network traffic is restricted, approved, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether it originates from users, servers, infrastructure, cloud, or hosted environments.
column5: Which outgoing traffic controls are used? Select one or more than one.
column6: Select the controls used to restrict, inspect, or monitor traffic leaving the network or hosted environment.
column8: Default deny egress rules|Web proxy or secure web gateway|DNS filtering|Firewall egress allow-listing|NAT gateway controls|Data loss prevention|IDS/IPS inspection|Cloud security gateway|SIEM alerting|Periodic rule reviews|Other

column3: This chapter reviews how outbound network traffic is restricted, approved, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether it originates from users, servers, infrastructure, cloud, or hosted environments.
column5: Are systems restricted from direct Internet access unless explicitly approved?
column6: Servers, infrastructure devices, administrative networks, and sensitive zones should use controlled paths such as proxies, NAT gateways, approved repositories, update services, or approved external integrations.

column3: This chapter reviews how outbound network traffic is restricted, approved, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether it originates from users, servers, infrastructure, cloud, or hosted environments.
column5: Are outgoing traffic rules reviewed periodically?
column6: The review should confirm owner, business purpose, destination, protocol, port, expiry date, change reference, and continued need.

column3: This chapter reviews how outbound network traffic is restricted, approved, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether it originates from users, servers, infrastructure, cloud, or hosted environments.
column5: Are outgoing traffic logs reviewed or alerted for suspicious activity?
column6: Examples include malware callbacks, unusual destinations, unauthorized tunnels, blocked categories, excessive data transfers, traffic from sensitive zones, and connections to prohibited locations.

column3: This chapter reviews how outbound network traffic is restricted, approved, monitored, and evidenced regardless of whether it originates from users, servers, infrastructure, cloud, or hosted environments.
column5: Control - What evidence can you share with auditors to prove outgoing traffic is controlled?
column6: Typical evidence includes egress firewall rules, proxy policies, DNS filtering reports, NAT rules, web gateway logs, SIEM alerts, data loss prevention alerts, change tickets, and rule review records.

column5: Upload any policy, procedure, standard, diagram, or evidence you have in regards to this topic.
column6: This can include network security policies, architecture diagrams, configuration standards, firewall standards, logging procedures, Wi-Fi standards, physical security procedures, traffic flow diagrams, or firewall rule review records.
